content:
# Genesis Chapter 30 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 16v–17v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_030_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_030_page_002.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_030_page_003.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 26–27 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0030

---

**1** When Rachel saw that she had borne Jacob no children, Rachel was jealous of her sister. She said to Jacob, "Give me children, or else I will die."

**2** Jacob's anger was kindled against Rachel, and he said, "Am I in the place of God, who has withheld from you the fruit of the womb?"

**3** Then she said, "Here is my servant Bilhah; go in to her, that she may bear upon my knees, and I too may be built up through her."

**4** So she gave him her servant Bilhah as a wife, and Jacob went in to her.

**5** And Bilhah conceived and bore Jacob a son.

**6** Then Rachel said, "God has judged me, and has also heard my voice and given me a son." Therefore she called his name Dan.

**7** Rachel's servant Bilhah conceived again and bore Jacob a second son.

**8** And Rachel said, "With mighty wrestlings I have wrestled with my sister and have prevailed." So she called his name Naphtali.

**9** When Leah saw that she had ceased bearing, she took her servant Zilpah and gave her to Jacob as a wife.

**10** Then Leah's servant Zilpah bore Jacob a son.

**11** And Leah said, "Good fortune has come!" So she called his name Gad.

**12** Leah's servant Zilpah bore Jacob a second son.

**13** And Leah said, "Happy am I! For the daughters will call me happy." So she called his name Asher.

**14** In the days of wheat harvest Reuben went and found mandrakes in the field and brought them to his mother Leah. Then Rachel said to Leah, "Please give me some of your son's mandrakes."

**15** But she said to her, "Is it a small matter that you have taken away my husband? Would you take away my son's mandrakes also?" Rachel said, "Then he may lie with you tonight in exchange for your son's mandrakes."

**16** When Jacob came from the field in the evening, Leah went out to meet him and said, "You must come in to me, for I have hired you with my son's mandrakes." So he lay with her that night.

**17** And God listened to Leah, and she conceived and bore Jacob a fifth son.

**18** Leah said, "God has given me my wages because I gave my servant to my husband." So she called his name Issachar.

**19** And Leah conceived again and bore Jacob a sixth son.

**20** Then Leah said, "God has endowed me with a good endowment; now my husband will honor me, because I have borne him six sons." So she called his name Zebulun.

**21** Afterward she bore a daughter and called her name Dinah.

**22** Then God remembered Rachel, and God listened to her and opened her womb.

**23** She conceived and bore a son and said, "God has taken away my reproach."

**24** So she called his name Joseph, saying, "May the LORD add to me another son!"

**25** As soon as Rachel had borne Joseph, Jacob said to Laban, "Send me away, that I may go to my own place and to my own country.

**26** Give me my wives and my children for whom I have served you, and let me go, for you yourself know my service that I have rendered you."

**27** But Laban said to him, "If I have found favor in your eyes, I have learned by divination that the LORD has blessed me because of you."

**28** He said, "Name your wages, and I will give it."

**29** Jacob said to him, "You yourself know how I have served you, and how your livestock have fared with me.

**30** For you had little before I came, and it has increased abundantly, and the LORD has blessed you wherever I turned. But now, when shall I provide for my own household also?"

**31** He said, "What shall I give you?" Jacob said, "You shall not give me anything. If you will do this one thing for me, I will again pasture your flock and keep it:

**32** Let me pass through all your flock today, removing from it every speckled and spotted sheep and every black lamb, and the spotted and speckled among the goats, and these shall be my wages.

**33** So my righteousness will answer for me later, when you come to examine my wages: every one that is not speckled and spotted among the goats and black among the lambs, if found with me, shall be considered stolen."

**34** Laban said, "Good! Let it be as you have said."

**35** But that day Laban removed the male goats that were striped and spotted and all the female goats that were speckled and spotted, every one that had white on it, and every black lamb, and put them in the care of his sons.

**36** And he set a distance of three days' journey between himself and Jacob, while Jacob pastured the rest of Laban's flocks.

**37** Then Jacob took fresh rods of poplar and almond and plane trees, and peeled white streaks in them, exposing the white of the rods.

**38** He set the rods that he had peeled in front of the flocks in the troughs, that is, the watering troughs, where the flocks came to drink. And since they bred when they came to drink,

**39** the flocks bred in front of the rods and the flocks brought forth striped, speckled, and spotted young.

**40** And Jacob separated the lambs and set the faces of the flocks toward the striped and all the black in the flock of Laban. He put his own flocks apart and did not put them with Laban's flock.

**41** Whenever the stronger of the flock were breeding, Jacob would place the rods in the troughs before the eyes of the flock, that they might breed among the rods,

**42** but for the feebler of the flock he would not place them there. So the feebler would be Laban's and the stronger Jacob's.

**43** Thus the man grew exceedingly prosperous and had large flocks, female servants and male servants, and camels and donkeys.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of five manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 30 spans Folios 16v–17v across three pages.

   **Folio 16v** (GEN_030_page_001.jpg): The verso of Folio 16, presented in inverted orientation per WSRP imaging convention. This page carries the end of Genesis 29 in its upper portion — specifically the births and naming of Leah's first four sons — and then continues into Genesis 30:1 at a visible paragraph break. The Genesis 30 material on this folio covers approximately verses 1 through 16: Rachel's jealousy and ultimatum to Jacob (vv. 1–2), the arrangement with Bilhah as surrogate (vv. 3–4), the births of Dan and Naphtali through Bilhah (vv. 5–8), Leah's counter-arrangement with Zilpah (vv. 9–10), the births of Gad and Asher through Zilpah (vv. 11–13), the mandrakes episode initiated by Reuben's discovery (vv. 14–15), and Leah's hiring of Jacob for the night (v. 16). The three-column layout continues with Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks intact. Masorah parva is visible in the inter-column margins.

   **Folio 17r** (GEN_030_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 17, with the folio number "17" visible in the upper margin. This folio carries approximately Genesis 30:17 through 30:38: Leah's births of Issachar (vv. 17–18), Zebulun (vv. 19–20), and Dinah (v. 21); God's remembering of Rachel and the birth of Joseph (vv. 22–24); Jacob's request to depart and the wage negotiation with Laban (vv. 25–34); Laban's removal of the striped and spotted animals (vv. 35–36); and Jacob's peeled-rod technique through the first placement of the rods at the watering troughs (vv. 37–38). The text is clear throughout.

   **Folio 17v** (GEN_030_page_003.jpg): The verso of Folio 17, again in inverted orientation. This page carries Genesis 30:39 through 30:43 and then continues into the opening verses of Genesis 31. The Genesis 30 material covers the conclusion of the breeding-at-the-rods episode: the flocks producing striped and spotted young (v. 39), Jacob's separation of the flocks (v. 40), his selective placement of rods for the stronger animals (vv. 41–42), and the concluding summary of Jacob's prosperity (v. 43).

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two pages were read directly.

   **Page 26** (GEN_030_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 26 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0026). The chapter marker "CAP. XXVII" is visible in the left margin. This page carries the opening of Genesis 30 and extends through the mandrake episode and into the birth of Joseph. The continuous uncial Greek text covers the surrogate arrangements with Bilhah and Zilpah, the four handmaid births, the mandrakes exchange, the births of Issachar, Zebulun, and Dinah, and the birth of Joseph. The three-column layout of Vaticanus continues throughout with the characteristic undivided word-stream.

   **Page 27** (GEN_030_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 27 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0027). The chapter marker "CAP. XXXIII" is visible in the left margin, marking the beginning of a new LXX chapter division. This page carries Jacob's negotiation with Laban, the spotted-flock arrangement, Laban's manipulation of the flocks, Jacob's peeled-rod technique, the selective breeding program, Jacob's great prosperity, and then continues into Genesis 31. The Greek text remains legible throughout.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "Give me children, or else I will die" — הָבָה לִי בָנִים וְאִם אַיִן מֵתָה אָנֹכִי (v. 1)**

Rachel's cry in verse 1 is one of the most raw and desperate utterances in Genesis. The Hebrew is stark: הָבָה לִי בָנִים (havah li vanim — "give me sons!") followed by the conditional וְאִם אַיִן מֵתָה אָנֹכִי (ve'im ayin metah anokhi — "and if not, I am dead / I will die"). The word מֵתָה (metah) is a participle or perfect used as a present statement of certain consequence — "I am as good as dead," "I will die." This is not hyperbole so much as a theological and social statement: in the ancient Near East, a woman's honor, security, and often her very identity were bound to her children. Barrenness was socially and spiritually devastating. The formal translation renders this literally: "Give me children, or else I will die." The LXX (Vaticanus) renders δός μοι τέκνα· εἰ δὲ μή, τελευτήσω ἐγώ (dos moi tekna; ei de mē, teleutēsō egō — "Give me children; but if not, I will die"), consistent with the MT.

**2. "Am I in the place of God?" — הֲתַחַת אֱלֹהִים אָנֹכִי (v. 2)**

Jacob's response to Rachel uses the phrase הֲתַחַת אֱלֹהִים אָנֹכִי (hatachat Elohim anokhi — "Am I in the place of God?"). The preposition תַּחַת (tachat) means "under, beneath, in the place of." Jacob is saying: I am not God's substitute; it is God who has closed your womb, not I. This is a theologically significant moment — Jacob both deflects responsibility and implicitly acknowledges divine sovereignty over fertility. The LXX renders μὴ ἀντὶ θεοῦ ἐγώ εἰμι (mē anti theou egō eimi — "Am I instead of God?"), using ἀντί (anti — "instead of, in the place of"), consistent with the MT. The formal translation renders "Am I in the place of God?" preserving the spatial idiom of the Hebrew.

**3. "Bear upon my knees" — וְתֵלֵד עַל בִּרְכַּי (v. 3)**

The phrase וְתֵלֵד עַל בִּרְכַּי (veteled al birkay — "and she will bear upon my knees") is the ancient idiom for the adoption-through-surrogacy ritual. The child would be placed upon the adopting mother's knees at birth, signifying that she received the child as her own. The same idiom appears in Genesis 50:23 (Joseph's grandchildren are born upon his knees) and Job 3:12 (Job asks why there were knees to receive him). The formal translation renders "that she may bear upon my knees" — preserving the literal idiom rather than paraphrasing it as "so that the child may count as mine." The LXX renders καὶ τέξεται ἐπὶ τῶν γονάτων μου (kai texetai epi tōn gonatōn mou — "and she shall bear upon my knees"), consistent with the MT.

**4. "I too may be built up through her" — וְאִבָּנֶה גַם אָנֹכִי מִמֶּנָּה (v. 3)**

Rachel's second clause — וְאִבָּנֶה גַם אָנֹכִי מִמֶּנָּה (ve'ibbaneh gam anokhi mimmenah — "and I too may be built up / built through her") — contains a significant wordplay. The verb בָּנָה (banah, "to build") is the root of both בֵּן (ben, "son") and of the concept of "building a household." This same wordplay appears in Genesis 16:2, where Sarai says "perhaps I shall be built up through her" regarding Hagar. To "be built up" through a servant's child is to establish one's house, one's lineage, one's standing. The formal translation renders "I too may be built up through her," preserving the building idiom. The LXX renders καὶ τεκνοποιήσομαι κἀγὼ ἐξ αὐτῆς (kai teknopoiēsomai kagō ex autēs — "and I too shall have children by her"), which unpacks the idiom rather than preserving it.

**5. Dan — "God has judged me" — דָּנַנִּי אֱלֹהִים (v. 6)**

The name Dan (דָּן) is connected by Rachel to the verb דִּין / דָּן (din/dan, "to judge, to vindicate"). Rachel's speech — דָּנַנִּי אֱלֹהִים (dananni Elohim — "God has judged me / vindicated me") — is the naming etymology. The verb here carries the sense of legal vindication: God has heard Rachel's case and ruled in her favor. The formal translation renders "God has judged me" rather than "vindicated" to preserve the primary lexical range of din/dan and to make the name's etymology transparent. The LXX renders ἔκρινέν μου ὁ θεός (ekrinen mou ho theos — "God has judged me"), consistent with the MT.

**6. Naphtali — "Mighty wrestlings of God" — נַפְתּוּלֵי אֱלֹהִים נִפְתַּלְתִּי (v. 8)**

The name Naphtali (נַפְתָּלִי) derives from the root פָּתַל (pathal, "to twist, to wrestle, to struggle"). Rachel's naming speech — נַפְתּוּלֵי אֱלֹהִים נִפְתַּלְתִּי עִם אֲחֹתִי (naphtuley Elohim niphalti im akoti — "with wrestlings of God I have wrestled with my sister") — uses both the abstract noun form (נַפְתּוּלֵי, naphtulei, "wrestlings of") and the niphal first-person perfect of the same root. The phrase נַפְתּוּלֵי אֱלֹהִים (naphtulei Elohim) uses Elohim as an intensifier in the Hebrew superlative idiom — "divine wrestlings" meaning "mighty / extreme wrestlings." The formal translation renders "With mighty wrestlings I have wrestled with my sister," using "mighty" to convey the superlative force of the Elohim genitive without over-theologizing it. The LXX renders συνελάβετό μοι ὁ θεός, καὶ συνεστράφην πρὸς τὴν ἀδελφήν μου (synelabeto moi ho theos, kai synestraphēn pros tēn adelphēn mou — "God helped me and I turned to my sister"), which is a notably different rendering, interpreting rather than translating the wrestling idiom.

**7. Gad — "Good fortune" — בָּא גָד (v. 11)**

The naming of Gad (גָּד) presents a well-known Qere/Ketiv difficulty. The Ketiv (written form) reads בָּגָד (vagad), which could be parsed as the preposition ב + גָּד ("with fortune") or as the verb בָּגַד (bagad, "to betray/act treacherously") — a reading that seems inapplicable to the birth announcement context. The Qere (read form) reads בָּא גָד (ba' gad — "fortune has come!" or "good fortune!"). The word גָּד (gad) is cognate with the Akkadian gadu/gaddu and refers to fortune, luck, or destiny — the same root underlies the deity "Gad" worshipped as the god of fortune (cf. Isaiah 65:11). The formal translation follows the Qere: "Good fortune has come!" The LXX renders ἐν τύχῃ (en tychē — "in/with good fortune"), supporting the Qere reading. This translation decision follows the established reading tradition.

**8. Asher — "Happy am I" — בְּאָשְׁרִי כִּי אִשְּׁרוּנִי בָנוֹת (v. 13)**

The name Asher (אָשֵׁר) derives from the root אָשַׁר (ashar, "to go straight, to be happy, to be blessed"). Leah's naming speech — בְּאָשְׁרִי כִּי אִשְּׁרוּנִי בָנוֹת (be'oshri ki ishsheruni vanot — "in my happiness, for the daughters will call me happy") — contains a triple play on the root: the noun אָשֶׁר (osher, "happiness"), the piel verb אִשֵּׁר (issher, "to call happy/blessed"), and the name Asher itself. The formal translation renders "Happy am I! For the daughters will call me happy," making the wordplay available to the English reader. The phrase "the daughters will call me happy" is the exact same construction as Ruth 3:17 and Proverbs 31:28 (where children "rise up and call her blessed"). The LXX renders ἐν εὐτυχίᾳ ὅτι μακαριοῦσίν με αἱ γυναῖκες (en eutychiai hoti makariousin me hai gynaikes — "in good fortune, for the women will call me blessed"), consistent with the MT.

**9. The Mandrakes — דוּדָאִים (duda'im) — (v. 14)**

The word דוּדָאִים (duda'im — "mandrakes") refers to the plant Mandragora officinarum, whose forked root resembles a human form and whose berries were widely believed in the ancient world to be an aphrodisiac and fertility agent. The word may derive from the root דּוֹד (dod, "love, beloved") — hence "love-apples" in some older translations. The mandrake grows in the wild and its fruit ripens around wheat harvest time (late spring in Canaan), which is consistent with the seasonal reference in verse 14. The formal translation uses "mandrakes" throughout — the standard English botanical term, well-established in biblical translation tradition. To render it "love-apples" or "love-plants" would introduce anachronistic or overly romantic connotations. The LXX renders μανδραγόραι (mandragorai), a Greek transliteration that became the source of the English "mandrake" itself. The narrative irony is sharp: Rachel, who acquires the fertility plant, remains barren, while Leah, who yields the mandrakes, immediately conceives again (v. 17) — suggesting that the story subverts rather than endorses the folk fertility belief.

**10. Issachar — "My wages" — שְׂכָרִי (v. 18)**

The name Issachar (יִשָּׂשכָר) is etymologically connected by Leah to שָׂכָר (sakhar, "wages, hire, reward"). Leah's naming speech — נָתַן אֱלֹהִים שְׂכָרִי (natan Elohim sekhari — "God has given my wages / my hire") — refers to the "hiring" of Jacob in verse 16 with the mandrakes. The name Issachar thus encodes the commercial language of the mandrake transaction — Leah purchased a night with Jacob and received a son as the divine wage. The formal translation renders "God has given me my wages," preserving the economic imagery. The LXX renders δέδωκέν μοι ὁ θεὸς τὸν μισθόν μου (dedōken moi ho theos ton misthon mou — "God has given me my wages"), consistent with the MT.

**11. Zebulun — "Endowment" and "Honor" — זָבַד / זָבַל (v. 20)**

The name Zebulun (זְבוּלוּן) is connected in Leah's speech to two related but distinct roots: זָבַד (zavad, "to endow, to gift") and זָבַל (zaval, "to honor, to dwell, to exalt"). Leah says זְבָדַנִי אֱלֹהִים אֹתִי זֵבֶד טוֹב הַפַּעַם יִזְבְּלֵנִי אִישִׁי (zevadani Elohim oti zevad tov hapa'am yizbeleni ishi — "God has endowed me with a good endowment; this time my husband will honor/dwell with me"). The first verb (zavad) and the second verb (zaval) are distinct. The formal translation renders both: "God has endowed me with a good endowment; now my husband will honor me," preserving both etymological threads. The LXX renders δεδώρηταί μοι ὁ θεὸς δῶρον καλόν· νῦν αἱρετιεῖ με ὁ ἀνήρ μου (dedōrētai moi ho theos dōron kalon; nyn hairetiei me ho anēr mou — "God has given me a good gift; now my husband will prefer/choose me"), which renders zaval as "prefer/choose" rather than "honor."

**12. Joseph — "May he add" — יֹסֵף יְהוָה לִי בֵּן אַחֵר (v. 24)**

The name Joseph (יוֹסֵף, Yosef) is connected by Rachel to two wordplays simultaneously. First, the verb אָסַף (asaph, "to gather, to take away") — God "has taken away" (asaph) her reproach (v. 23). Second, the verb יָסַף (yasaph, "to add, to do again") — "may the LORD add (yoseph) to me another son" (v. 24). The name Yosef phonically evokes both: asaph (removal of shame) and yasaph (addition of blessing). The formal translation preserves this double meaning across two consecutive verses: "God has taken away my reproach" (v. 23) and "May the LORD add to me another son!" (v. 24). The use of "the LORD" (YHWH) in Rachel's prayer — the first time YHWH appears in connection with Rachel directly — is significant: Rachel, who has been associated primarily with Elohim throughout this chapter, now invokes the covenant name. The LXX renders προσθέτω μοι ὁ θεὸς υἱὸν ἕτερον (prostheto moi ho theos huion heteron — "may God add to me another son"), using Elohim rather than YHWH — a textual difference noted below.

**13. Laban's divination — נִחַשְׁתִּי (v. 27)**

Laban's statement in verse 27 contains the verb נִחַשׁ (nicash, "to divine, to practice divination, to observe omens"). He says נִחַשְׁתִּי וַיְבָרְכֵנִי יְהוָה בִּגְלָלֶךָ (nichashti vayevarkheni YHWH biglalekha — "I have divined / learned by omens that the LORD has blessed me because of you"). This is a remarkable admission: Laban, the Aramean, uses a verb associated with divination (elsewhere condemned in Deuteronomy 18:10) to discover that the God of Israel (YHWH) has blessed him. The formal translation renders "I have learned by divination" — preserving the specific verbal root rather than the vaguer "I have found" or "I have noticed." The LXX renders οἰωνισάμην (oiōnisamēn — "I have taken an augury, I have observed omens"), consistent with the MT in specifying divination, not mere observation.

**14. The Peeled Rods — the selective breeding technique (vv. 37–42)**

Jacob's method of producing striped and spotted offspring involves peeling white streaks into green rods of poplar, almond, and plane trees and placing these rods before the animals at the watering troughs during breeding. The ancient logic — that visual stimulation during conception would affect the offspring's appearance — reflects a folk-biological belief widespread in antiquity (cf. Aristotle's references to maternal impression). The text narrates the technique without editorial commentary; it is only in Genesis 31:10–12 that Jacob reveals (in his dream account to Rachel) that the success of the selective breeding was due to divine intervention rather than the rod technique itself. The formal translation renders the technique faithfully as described, using the specific tree names:

- **לִבְנֶה** (livneh) — poplar (likely Populus alba, the white poplar, whose name connects to לָבָן, lavan, "white" — the wordplay with Laban's name may be intentional). The LXX renders ῥάβδον στυρακίνην (rhabdon styrakinēn — "a rod of storax tree"), a different identification.
- **לוּז** (luz) — almond (Amygdalus communis). The name Luz is also the pre-Bethel name of the place Jacob renamed (Genesis 28:19). The LXX renders κάρυον (karyon — "nut tree").
- **עַרְמוֹן** (armon) — plane tree (Platanus orientalis, the Oriental plane). The LXX renders πλάτανον (platanon — "plane tree"), consistent with the MT.

The formal translation uses standard English botanical identifications — poplar, almond, and plane tree — following the MT's identifications.

**15. "Stronger" and "feebler" of the flock — קְשֻׁרוֹת / עֲטֻפִים (vv. 41–42)**

In the selective breeding scheme, Jacob places the rods only before the "stronger" (קְשֻׁרוֹת, qeshurot, "bound together, vigorous, in heat") animals and not before the "feebler" (עֲטֻפִים, atufim, "weak, feeble, languid") ones. The result is that the stronger animals produce the spotted offspring that become Jacob's, while the feebler produce pure animals that become Laban's. The word qeshurot is related to the root קָשַׁר (qashar, "to bind, to tie") — in this context it describes animals in the peak of vitality and breeding condition, their vigor likened to a binding energy. The formal translation renders "the stronger of the flock" and "the feebler of the flock," preserving the contrast. The LXX renders τὰ πρόβατα τὰ πολυτελῆ (ta probata ta polytele — "the excellent/valuable sheep") and τὰ ἄσημα (ta asema — "the insignificant/unmarked ones"), focusing on quality rather than strength.

**16. "Grew exceedingly prosperous" — וַיִּפְרֹץ הָאִישׁ מְאֹד מְאֹד (v. 43)**

The closing verse's summary of Jacob's wealth uses the verb פָּרַץ (paratz, "to break out, to burst forth, to spread abundantly") — the same root connected to the name Perez (פֶּרֶץ, the son of Judah and Tamar, Genesis 38:29) and the same verb used of Israel's spread in Exodus 1:12. The doubling of מְאֹד מְאֹד (me'od me'od — "very very / exceedingly") intensifies the statement. The formal translation renders "grew exceedingly prosperous," maintaining the force of the doubled adverb. The LXX renders ηὐξήθη ὁ ἄνθρωπος σφόδρα σφόδρα (ēuxēthē ho anthrōpos sphodra sphodra — "the man grew exceedingly exceedingly"), matching the MT's doubled intensifier.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 30

Pages 26–27 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text across both pages was legible. Key variants observed:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30:3 | וְאִבָּנֶה גַם אָנֹכִי ("I too may be built up") | καὶ τεκνοποιήσομαι κἀγὼ ἐξ αὐτῆς ("I too shall have children by her") | "I too may be built up through her" | LXX unpacks the building/son wordplay; formal translation preserves MT idiom |
| 30:6 | דָּנַנִּי אֱלֹהִים ("God has judged me") | ἔκρινέν μου ὁ θεός ("God has judged me") | "God has judged me" | MT and LXX agree |
| 30:8 | נַפְתּוּלֵי אֱלֹהִים ("wrestlings of God") | συνελάβετό μοι ὁ θεός ("God helped me") | "mighty wrestlings" | LXX significantly reinterprets the wrestling idiom; formal translation follows MT |
| 30:11 | בָּא גָד (Qere: "fortune has come") | ἐν τύχῃ ("in/with good fortune") | "Good fortune has come!" | Both support Qere against Ketiv (בָּגָד); formal translation follows Qere |
| 30:20 | יִזְבְּלֵנִי אִישִׁי ("my husband will honor me") | νῦν αἱρετιεῖ με ὁ ἀνήρ μου ("my husband will prefer me") | "my husband will honor me" | Different rendering of zaval; formal translation follows MT's honor/dwell sense |
| 30:22 | וַיִּשְׁמַע אֵלֶיהָ אֱלֹהִים ("God listened to her") | εἰσήκουσεν αὐτῆς ὁ θεός ("God heard her") | "God listened to her" | MT and LXX agree in substance |
| 30:24 | יֹסֵף יְהוָה לִי ("May the LORD add to me") | προσθέτω μοι ὁ θεός ("may God add to me") | "May the LORD add to me" | LXX uses Elohim/theos; MT uses YHWH/LORD — formal translation follows MT's theologically significant use of the covenant name on Rachel's lips for the first time |
| 30:27 | נִחַשְׁתִּי ("I have divined") | οἰωνισάμην ("I have taken an augury") | "I have learned by divination" | MT and LXX agree that divination is specified; formal translation names the practice |
| 30:37 | לִבְנֶה ("poplar") | ῥάβδον στυρακίνην ("storax rod") | "poplar" | LXX identifies the tree differently; MT's livneh is likely white poplar (Populus alba); formal translation follows MT |
| 30:43 | מְאֹד מְאֹד ("very very / exceedingly") | σφόδρα σφόδρα ("exceedingly exceedingly") | "exceedingly prosperous" | MT and LXX agree on the doubled intensifier |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Tribal Birth Narrative: All Twelve (Minus Benjamin)**

Genesis 30 completes eleven of the twelve tribal ancestors of Israel — ten sons and one daughter are born in this chapter (Dan, Naphtali, Gad, Asher, Issachar, Zebulun, Dinah, and Joseph) alongside the four already born in Genesis 29 (Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah). Only Benjamin remains, whose birth is narrated in Genesis 35:18 and whose naming is shadowed by Rachel's death. The twelve-son structure is not merely genealogical — it is the scaffolding upon which the entire subsequent narrative of Israel is built. The formal translation preserves the careful patterning of the naming speeches: each name is etymologized through the mother's interpretive speech, and each interpretation reflects the mother's emotional and spiritual condition at that moment.

**The Competing Mothers: Leah, Rachel, Bilhah, Zilpah**

The birth narrative of Genesis 30 is structured around an intensifying competition between Leah and Rachel, conducted through the bodies of their respective handmaids Bilhah and Zilpah. The competitive dynamic is explicit in Rachel's speech at v. 8 ("I have wrestled with my sister") and in Leah's response to Rachel's request for mandrakes (v. 15 — "Is it a small matter that you have taken away my husband?"). The formal translation preserves the sharpness of Leah's rebuke in v. 15 — "Is it a small matter?" (הַמְעַט קַחְתֵּךְ אֶת אִישִׁי, hame'at qakhtekh et-ishi) — without softening it. This is a moment of open conflict between two women bound in a forced and unequal marriage, and the text records it without flinching.

**The Mandrake Irony: Fertility Reversed**

The mandrake episode (vv. 14–21) is constructed around a pointed irony: Rachel seeks the mandrakes — ancient fertility symbols — and trades her husband's company for them. But Rachel remains barren. It is Leah, who gave up the mandrakes, who immediately conceives again (v. 17 — "And God listened to Leah, and she conceived"). Then, finally, after all of this, "God remembered Rachel" (v. 22) — not because of the mandrakes, but because of his own sovereign decision. The narrative's placement of "God remembered Rachel" after the mandrake failure is unmistakable: human strategies for fertility are subverted; divine remembrance is the actual cause of Rachel's conception. The formal translation renders "God remembered Rachel" with the full weight of the verb זָכַר (zakhar, "to remember") — the same verb used of God "remembering" Noah (Genesis 8:1) and of God "remembering" his covenant (Exodus 2:24). Divine remembrance in the Hebrew Bible is not merely cognitive — it is salvific action.

**Rachel's First Use of YHWH**

Throughout Genesis 30, Rachel has prayed to and spoken of Elohim (vv. 6, 8, 23). But in the naming of Joseph (v. 24), she prays "May the LORD (YHWH) add to me another son!" This is Rachel's first use of the covenant name YHWH — and the LXX Vaticanus reads Elohim at this point, not YHWH. The formal translation follows the MT's YHWH here, as the Leningrad Codex clearly reads יְהוָה and the reading is well-supported in the Masoretic tradition. The shift from Elohim to YHWH in Rachel's final naming speech may mark a deepening of her relationship to the covenant God — the God who is not merely a divine power who "judges" and "remembers" but the personal covenant deity of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob who "adds." The LXX's harmonization to Elohim loses this nuance.

**Jacob's Prosperity as Providence**

The closing section of Genesis 30 (vv. 25–43) recounts Jacob's wage negotiation with Laban and his selective breeding program. The text sets up a tension: Laban acknowledges in v. 27 that YHWH has blessed him because of Jacob — and then immediately manipulates the terms to minimize Jacob's share. Jacob's counter-strategy with the peeled rods appears at first glance to be a piece of folk biology, but the subsequent revelation in Genesis 31:10–12 — where Jacob describes a dream in which the angel of God shows him striped and spotted males mounting the flock — reinterprets the rod technique as a vehicle of divine provision. The formal translation narrates vv. 37–43 as the text presents them, without anticipating Genesis 31's reinterpretation, allowing the narrative ambiguity to stand. The chapter closes not with a statement about Jacob's cleverness but with a summary of his prosperity — "he had large flocks, female servants and male servants, and camels and donkeys" — whose scope answers the destitution with which Jacob arrived at Laban's house in Genesis 29:1.
